text: How is the Nova Scotia Class 7 knowledge test divided?

options.text: Driving rules and road signs
Parking only and winter driving
Vehicle inspection and insurance
A vision test and a road test

explanation: The Class 7 knowledge test has two parts: driving rules and road signs. Each part has 20 multiple-choice questions.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, How to Apply for a Learner's Licence

text: How many questions must you answer correctly to pass each part of the Class 7 knowledge test?

options.text: 16 out of 20
12 out of 20
14 out of 20
18 out of 20

explanation: You need at least 16 correct answers on each 20-question part. Passing one part does not make up for failing the other.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, How to Apply for a Learner's Licence

text: How long do you have to complete each part of the online Class 7 knowledge test?

options.text: 30 minutes
15 minutes
20 minutes
60 minutes

explanation: Each part of the knowledge test must be completed within 30 minutes. The full simulator uses that timing for each section.
handbookSection: Official Class 7 Knowledge Test — How Long It Takes

text: What must happen after you pass the online Class 7 knowledge test before you can drive?

options.text: You must visit Registry of Motor Vehicles or Access Nova Scotia to finish licence requirements
You can drive immediately if you print the result
You only need to buy insurance
You must wait 30 days before applying

explanation: Passing the online test is not the licence by itself. You still need to complete the remaining learner licence requirements before driving.
handbookSection: Official Class 7 Knowledge Test — When You Pass the Knowledge Test

text: What is the minimum age to apply for a Nova Scotia Learner’s Licence (Class 7)?
explanation: A person must be at least 16 to apply for a Class 7 Learner’s Licence. Drivers who are 16 or 17 need parental or guardian consent.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Getting a Learner's Licence

text: What blood alcohol level must a learner driver maintain in Nova Scotia?

options.text: Zero
Below 0.03
Below 0.05
Below 0.08

explanation: Learner drivers must keep a zero blood alcohol level. This is stricter than the Criminal Code limit.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Learner's Licence Restrictions

text: What does a steady red traffic light mean?

options.text: Stop before entering the intersection
Stop only if traffic is coming
Slow down and proceed with caution
Yield without stopping

explanation: A red signal means all traffic facing it must stop at the marked stop line or before the crosswalk. Proceed only when legally allowed and safe.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

text: What does a steady yellow or amber traffic light mean?

options.text: Stop if you can do so safely
Speed up to clear the intersection
The signal is out of service
Pedestrians may not cross

explanation: A yellow or amber signal means you must stop before entering the intersection if you can stop safely. Never speed up to beat the red.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

text: What does a green traffic light allow you to do?

options.text: Proceed when safe, yielding where required
Proceed without checking for pedestrians
Ignore signs at the intersection
Turn left without yielding

explanation: A green light means you may go when safe, but you must still yield to pedestrians and vehicles already in the intersection.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

text: What does a flashing red traffic light mean?

options.text: Stop, then proceed only when safe
Slow down and keep going
The road is closed
Only trucks must stop

explanation: A flashing red light has the same meaning as a stop sign. Stop completely and do not proceed until it is safe.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Flashing Signals

text: What does a flashing yellow or amber traffic light mean?

options.text: Slow down and proceed with caution
Stop and wait for green
The signal applies only to pedestrians
Turn around

explanation: A flashing yellow or amber light has the meaning of a warning sign. Slow down and continue carefully.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Flashing Signals

text: If traffic lights are not working at an intersection, what should drivers do?

options.text: Treat it as a multi-stop intersection
Drive through if you are on the larger road
Stop only if another car is present
Wait until police arrive

explanation: When traffic signal lights are not working, treat the intersection as a multi-stop. Stop, then proceed when you have the right of way and it is safe.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

text: Who has the right of way at a marked or unmarked crosswalk?

options.text: Pedestrians in the crosswalk
The fastest vehicle
Only pedestrians at marked crosswalks
Vehicles turning right

explanation: Drivers must yield to pedestrians at intersections, whether the crosswalk is marked or unmarked. Always be ready to stop.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Pedestrians and Crosswalks

text: At an intersection, what must a driver turning left on a green light do?

options.text: Yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians in the path
Turn before oncoming traffic moves
Use the horn and continue
Stop until the light turns red

explanation: A driver turning left must yield to oncoming vehicles and pedestrians who may be in the path. Turn only when it is safe.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

text: When two vehicles arrive at an uncontrolled intersection at the same time, who yields?

options.text: The driver on the left yields to the driver on the right
The driver on the right yields to the driver on the left
The larger vehicle yields
The vehicle going straight always yields

explanation: When two or more vehicles reach an intersection at the same time, the vehicle on the left must yield. Courtesy never replaces checking that it is safe.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

text: What should you do when leaving a private driveway or parking lot?

options.text: Stop and yield to all vehicles and pedestrians
Enter traffic immediately if signalling
Yield only to vehicles from the left
Use your horn and proceed

explanation: Before entering a road from a driveway or parking lot, stop and yield to all road users. Move only when the way is clear.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

text: Who has right of way when a vehicle is already in an intersection?

options.text: The vehicle already in the intersection
The vehicle that arrived later from the right
The vehicle turning left
The vehicle with the newest licence

explanation: A vehicle already in the intersection has right of way over one that has not yet entered. You still must avoid a collision when possible.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

text: Where should you position your front wheels while waiting to turn left?

options.text: Straight ahead until you can complete the turn
Turned left toward the oncoming lane
Turned right toward the curb
It does not matter

explanation: Keep your wheels straight while waiting to turn left. If hit from behind, turned wheels can push you into oncoming traffic.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

text: When should you avoid changing lanes?

options.text: While moving through an intersection
On a straight road with lane markings
After checking mirrors and shoulder
Before entering an intersection

explanation: Make lane changes before entering an intersection. Changing lanes in an intersection is unsafe and can confuse other drivers.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

text: At a four-way stop, what should you do before proceeding?

options.text: Stop completely and go only when it is your turn and safe
Roll through if no one honks
Follow the closest vehicle without stopping
Proceed first if your vehicle is larger

explanation: At a multi-stop, every driver must stop completely and proceed by right-of-way order. If unsure, yield and communicate clearly.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

text: What must you do if a pedestrian remains in the street when the signal changes?

options.text: Allow the pedestrian to finish crossing safely
Honk so the pedestrian hurries
Drive around the pedestrian
Stop only if the pedestrian is in your lane

explanation: If a signal changes while a pedestrian is still crossing, drivers must allow the pedestrian to complete the crossing safely.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Pedestrians and Crosswalks

text: What should you do when a crossing guard holds a stop paddle toward your vehicle?

options.text: Stop and obey the crossing guard
Slow down but keep moving
Stop only in school hours
Proceed if no child is visible

explanation: Drivers must obey appointed crossing guards. A stop paddle facing your vehicle means stop.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Crosswalk Guards

text: What is the safe choice when passing a cyclist?

options.text: Leave plenty of space and pass only when safe
Pass closely so you spend less time beside them
Use the horn continuously
Pass on the shoulder whenever possible

explanation: Cyclists are vulnerable road users. Give space, check for oncoming traffic, and pass only when it can be done safely.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Sharing the Road with Bicycles

text: On a road with a posted speed limit of 60 km/h or less, what should you do when approaching from behind a stopped transit bus signalling to re-enter traffic?

options.text: Yield if it is safe to let the bus re-enter traffic
Always pass immediately on the left
Treat it the same as a school bus with red flashing lights
Yield only after the bus has fully entered your lane

explanation: The handbook update says drivers approaching from the rear must yield to a stopped transit bus that signals to re-enter traffic when the posted limit is 60 km/h or less and yielding is safe. This rule does not apply to school buses.
handbookSection: Introduction — Driver's Handbook Updates, Yield to Transit Buses

text: What is the safest approach around large trucks?

options.text: Stay out of blind spots and leave extra room
Follow closely to reduce wind resistance
Cut in front after passing
Assume the driver can always see you

explanation: Large vehicles have bigger blind spots and need more space to stop and turn. Keep visible and avoid cutting in.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Trucker's Blind Spots

text: When meeting farm or slow-moving equipment, what should you do?

options.text: Slow down and pass only when legal and safe
Pass immediately on any shoulder
Flash headlights until it moves off the road
Follow within one car length

explanation: Slow-moving vehicles may turn wide or move unpredictably. Reduce speed and pass only when you have clear sight distance and it is legal.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Encountering Other Road Users

text: What should you do when an emergency vehicle approaches with flashing lights and siren?

options.text: Move to the right edge or curb and stop
Speed up to stay ahead
Stop in the middle of the lane
Follow it through traffic

explanation: When an emergency vehicle approaches, move to the right edge or curb and stop completely. Stay stopped until it passes or an officer directs you.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Emergency Vehicles

text: How close may you follow an emergency fire vehicle responding to a call?

options.text: Never within 150 metres
At least 30 metres
One car length
As close as traffic allows

explanation: You must never follow an emergency fire vehicle within 150 metres. Leave the route clear for emergency response.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Emergency Vehicles

text: How close may you drive or park to an emergency vehicle stopped to assist at a collision?

options.text: Not within 150 metres
Not within 15 metres
Only outside city limits
There is no distance rule

explanation: The handbook says never drive or park within 150 metres of an emergency vehicle stopped to investigate a collision or give assistance.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Emergency Vehicles

text: What speed limit applies in a posted school zone when children are present and the approaching limit is 50 km/h?

options.text: 30 km/h
40 km/h
50 km/h
60 km/h

explanation: When children are present in such a school zone, the maximum speed is 30 km/h. The rule applies whatever the time of day.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, School and Playground Signs

text: In a school zone where the approaching speed limit is higher than 50 km/h, what is the maximum speed when children are present?

options.text: 50 km/h
30 km/h
60 km/h
The posted highway speed

explanation: For school zones with an approaching limit higher than 50 km/h, the school-zone maximum is 50 km/h when children are present.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, School and Playground Signs

text: What should you do when weather or traffic makes the posted speed unsafe?

options.text: Drive slower than the posted limit
Drive exactly the posted limit
Use hazard lights and maintain speed
Follow the closest vehicle

explanation: The posted limit is a maximum for ideal conditions. Slow down when weather, visibility, traffic, or road surface conditions require it.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Speed Limits

text: What is a good minimum following distance in normal conditions?

options.text: At least two seconds behind the vehicle ahead
Half a second
Exactly one car length at any speed
Close enough to read the plate

explanation: Use at least a two-second following distance in good conditions. Increase the space when conditions are poor.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Two-Second Rule

text: When should you increase your following distance?

options.text: In rain, snow, darkness, heavy traffic, or when following large vehicles
Only on sunny days
Only below 30 km/h
Never if you have ABS brakes

explanation: Poor visibility, slippery roads, heavy traffic, and large vehicles all increase stopping needs. More space gives you time to react.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Two-Second Rule

text: Where is parking normally prohibited?

options.text: In front of a fire hydrant
In a marked parking stall
In your own driveway
Beside a curb where signs allow it

explanation: Never park where you block emergency access, such as in front of a fire hydrant. Obey all posted parking controls.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Parking and Stopping

text: Before opening a vehicle door on the traffic side, what should you do?

options.text: Check for vehicles and cyclists
Open it quickly so others react
Leave it open if loading
Only check the mirror after opening

explanation: Check mirrors and shoulder before opening a door. Opening into a cyclist or vehicle path can cause a serious collision.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Parking and Stopping

text: When parking on a hill facing downhill, which way should you turn your front wheels?

options.text: Toward the curb or roadside
Away from the curb
Straight ahead only
Toward the centre line

explanation: Turn wheels toward the curb when parking downhill. This helps the curb stop the vehicle if it rolls.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Parking on a Hill

text: When is passing another vehicle unsafe?

options.text: When you cannot see far enough ahead
On a straight road with a clear passing lane
When markings and signs allow it
After checking mirrors and shoulder

explanation: Do not pass unless you have enough clear sight distance and the law allows it. If in doubt, wait.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Overtaking and Passing

text: What should you do before changing lanes to pass?

options.text: Signal, check mirrors, check blind spot, then move when safe
Signal after you begin moving
Assume other drivers will slow down
Move first and check after

explanation: A safe lane change includes signalling, mirror checks, a shoulder check, and moving only when the space is clear.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Signalling and Lane Changes

text: What should you do if another vehicle is stopped at a crosswalk for a pedestrian?

options.text: Do not pass the stopped vehicle
Pass slowly on the left
Pass only if you cannot see the pedestrian
Honk and continue

explanation: Never pass a vehicle stopped at a crosswalk for a pedestrian. A pedestrian may be hidden from your view.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Overtaking and Passing

text: When entering a highway from an acceleration lane, what should you do?

options.text: Match traffic speed, signal, and merge when safe
Stop at the end of the lane
Force other drivers to brake
Enter at very low speed

explanation: Use the acceleration lane to build speed, signal, and merge into a safe gap. Avoid stopping unless traffic conditions require it.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Controlled-Access Highways

text: What should you do if you miss your highway exit?

options.text: Continue to the next exit
Back up on the shoulder
Make a U-turn through the median
Stop and reverse carefully

explanation: Never back up or make sudden illegal moves on a highway. Continue to the next exit and return safely.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Controlled-Access Highways

text: Why should you avoid driving for long periods beside another vehicle on a multi-lane road?

options.text: It can block escape space and keep you in a blind spot
It saves fuel only at low speed
It is required by law
It helps other drivers merge

explanation: Keeping space around your vehicle gives you options if something changes. Avoid lingering in another driver’s blind spot.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Defensive Driving and Blind Spots

text: What does a solid yellow line on your side of the centre line usually mean?

options.text: Do not pass
Passing is always allowed
The lane is for buses only
Parking is allowed

explanation: A solid yellow line on your side means passing is restricted. Wait for legal markings and clear sight distance.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: What do white lane lines normally separate?

options.text: Traffic moving in the same direction
Traffic moving in opposite directions
Parking spaces only
Railway crossings only

explanation: White lane lines separate traffic moving in the same direction. Yellow markings are used to separate opposing traffic.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: What does a painted stop line show?

options.text: Where to stop at a sign or signal
Where to begin accelerating
Where pedestrians must wait
Where parking starts

explanation: A stop line marks where drivers should stop for a stop sign, traffic signal, or crossing control. Stop before the line.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: What should you check before driving?

options.text: Mirrors, seat, lights, tires, and that passengers are secure
Only the radio volume
Only the fuel level
Nothing if the car started

explanation: A safe pre-drive check includes visibility, controls, lights, tires, and occupant safety. Small checks prevent avoidable problems.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Vehicle Maintenance

text: Why should all occupants wear seat belts?

options.text: They greatly reduce injury risk in a collision
They are only useful on highways
They replace airbags
They are optional in the back seat

explanation: Seat belts help keep occupants in place and reduce injury in crashes. Airbags are designed to work with seat belts, not replace them.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Passenger Safety

text: What should you do if your brakes fail?

options.text: Pump the brake pedal, then apply the parking brake gently if needed
Shift to park immediately while the vehicle is moving
Turn off the engine and steer sharply onto the shoulder
Keep driving slowly until you reach a service station

explanation: Chapter 5 says to pump the brake pedal several times to build pressure. If that fails, apply the parking brake gently while holding the release button, then get help once stopped.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Brake Failure

text: What should you do first when rain, snow, or fog reduces visibility?

options.text: Slow down and increase following distance
Use high beams in fog
Follow tail lights closely
Drive at the posted maximum

explanation: Poor visibility requires lower speed and more space. Use appropriate lights and avoid following too closely.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Rain, Snow, and Fog

text: Why are bridges and overpasses hazardous in winter?

options.text: They can freeze before other road surfaces
They are always salted first
They have no speed limits
They make tires warmer

explanation: Bridges and overpasses are exposed to cold air above and below, so they can become icy sooner than regular pavement.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Winter Driving

text: What is hydroplaning?

options.text: When tires ride on water and lose contact with the road
When brakes become too hot
When steering locks at low speed
When headlights reflect from snow

explanation: Hydroplaning happens when water builds between tires and the road. Slow down in heavy rain and avoid sudden steering or braking.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Rain

text: What should you do when meeting oncoming traffic at night?

options.text: Dim high beams to avoid glare
Keep high beams on to see better
Look directly into oncoming headlights
Turn headlights off briefly

explanation: Dim your high beams for oncoming traffic so you do not blind other drivers. Look toward the right edge if glare bothers you.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Nighttime Driving

text: Why should you reduce speed at night?

options.text: You can only see as far as your headlights reach
Night air makes tires slippery
Speed limits double at night
Other vehicles have no right of way

explanation: At night, visibility is limited by headlights and glare. Drive at a speed that lets you stop within the distance you can see.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Nighttime Driving

text: What is the safest rule about driving after drinking or using impairing drugs?

options.text: Do not drive
Drive slowly
Drink coffee first
Open the windows

explanation: Alcohol and drugs impair judgment, reaction time, and coordination. The safe choice is not to drive.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drinking and Driving

text: Can prescription or over-the-counter medicine affect driving?

options.text: Yes, some medicines can impair driving
No, only illegal drugs matter
Only if mixed with food
Only at night

explanation: Some legal medicines can cause drowsiness or slowed reactions. Read labels and ask a health professional when unsure.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drug Impairment

text: Why is planning a sober ride before going out important?

options.text: Impaired judgment can make bad choices more likely later
It reduces fuel cost
It lets you ignore licence restrictions
It is only needed for long trips

explanation: Impairment makes it harder to judge risk later. Plan a sober ride, transit, taxi, or staying over before drinking or using impairing drugs.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Being a Responsible Host or Hostess

text: What does a regulatory sign tell drivers?

options.text: A law or rule they must obey
A scenic route suggestion
A weather forecast
A restaurant location

explanation: Regulatory signs state legal requirements such as stop, yield, speed, parking, and movement controls. Drivers must obey them.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

text: What do warning signs normally tell drivers?

options.text: A hazard or change in road conditions ahead
A mandatory parking rule
The price of fuel nearby
A licence office location

explanation: Warning signs prepare drivers for hazards such as curves, crossings, lane changes, and slippery surfaces. Slow and adjust early.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What do orange signs usually warn about?

options.text: Construction or work zones
Hospital services
Tourist attractions
Regular route numbers

explanation: Orange signs identify construction and work zones. Watch for workers, equipment, lane shifts, and temporary traffic control.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What does this sign mean?
imageAlt: Flashing arrow board with a single directional arrow

options.text: The lane ahead is closed or obstructed; move over and pass only on the side shown by the arrow.
The lane ahead is closed or obstructed; it is safe to pass the flashing light unit on either side.
A straight bar of lamps is caution mode, warning of a hazard without saying it is safe to pass.
Blasting is taking place on or near the road and traffic may be required to stop.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that flashing light units warn of lane closures, obstructions, or active hazards. In this case, the lane ahead is closed or obstructed; move over and pass only on the side shown by the arrow.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Flashing Light Units

text: What does this sign indicate?
imageAlt: Flashing arrow board with two arrow heads

options.text: Passing with care is permitted in both directions when the centre line is broken.
The lane ahead is closed or obstructed; it is safe to pass the flashing light unit on either side.
A bump or rough road is ahead; slow down.
The maximum speed limit changes ahead; be ready to adjust your speed.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that flashing light units warn of lane closures, obstructions, or active hazards. In this case, the lane ahead is closed or obstructed; it is safe to pass the flashing light unit on either side.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Flashing Light Units

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Orange work-zone warning sign for blasting activity

options.text: Tar has been sprayed on the road ahead; the surface may be slippery and tar may stick to your vehicle.
The road has returned to normal and the work-zone impact has ended.
Blasting is taking place on or near the road and traffic may be required to stop.
Traffic may be stopped by traffic control persons ahead; slow gradually to avoid rear-end collisions.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, blasting is taking place on or near the road and traffic may be required to stop.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What action does this sign require?
imageAlt: Yellow warning sign showing an opening bridge

options.text: The road may be slippery when wet.
A steep decline is ahead; you may need to use a lower gear.
A stop sign is ahead.
A bridge that can open for boats is ahead.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a bridge that can open for boats is ahead.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What does this road marking indicate?
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with one broken yellow line and one solid yellow line

options.text: Passing is permitted only when the broken yellow line is on your side and conditions are safe.
Do not litter from your vehicle or along the roadway.
Do not pass; the sign creates an orderly flow before a merge or where passing is unsafe.
Do not pass other vehicles.

explanation: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, passing is permitted only when the broken yellow line is on your side and conditions are safe.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: How should drivers read this pavement marking?
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with a broken yellow centre line

options.text: Hazard marker lines slope left; drive to the left of the sign.
Passing with care is permitted in both directions when the centre line is broken.
Hazard marker lines slope right; drive to the right of the sign.
A hidden intersection is ahead; side-road drivers may not clearly see approaching traffic.

explanation: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, passing with care is permitted in both directions when the centre line is broken.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Yellow diamond warning sign for a bump or rough road

options.text: A railway crossing is at the road; look and listen for trains.
There is a height difference between lanes that could catch a tire and cause loss of control.
A bump or rough road is ahead; slow down.
Do not enter this road or lane.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, a bump or rough road is ahead; slow down.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What action does this sign require?
imageAlt: Warning sign showing an upcoming speed limit change

options.text: Do not pass other vehicles.
Do not cross continuous double solid yellow lines to pass in either direction.
Drive to the right of this sign or obstruction.
The maximum speed limit changes ahead; be ready to adjust your speed.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, the maximum speed limit changes ahead; be ready to adjust your speed.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What does this sign mean?
imageAlt: Black and yellow chevron arrow sign

options.text: Chevron arrows guide drivers around a sharp curve.
Keep to the right of the traffic island or obstruction.
A straight bar of lamps is caution mode, warning of a hazard without saying it is safe to pass.
A solid yellow line marks the left edge of a divided highway; seeing it on the wrong side may mean you are travelling the wrong way.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, chevron arrows guide drivers around a sharp curve.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What does this pavement marking mean?
imageAlt: Pavement diagram showing a climbing lane

options.text: A playground is ahead; reduce speed and watch for children.
A climbing lane lets faster traffic pass slower vehicles; keep right unless passing.
Prepare for unusual road conditions or construction traffic moving at a different speed.
A right curve is ahead and the posted advisory speed indicates a safe speed for the turn.

explanation: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, a climbing lane lets faster traffic pass slower vehicles; keep right unless passing.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Orange work-zone sign showing construction ahead distance

options.text: No turns are permitted.
Do not make a U-turn to travel in the opposite direction.
Construction starts 1.5 km ahead; choose another route or prepare for delays.
Traffic may travel only in the direction shown by the arrow.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, construction starts 1.5 km ahead; choose another route or prepare for delays.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What action does this sign require?
imageAlt: Orange work-zone warning sign for construction traffic entering from the right

options.text: A playground is ahead; reduce speed and watch for children.
A right curve is ahead and the posted advisory speed indicates a safe speed for the turn.
Drivers in this lane must turn right at the intersection.
Construction traffic may enter or leave the road on the right and may be slower than through traffic.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, construction traffic may enter or leave the road on the right and may be slower than through traffic.

Drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn left.

explanation: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn left.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

text: What does this sign mean?
imageAlt: Orange human-activity sign for survey work

options.text: Survey work is being done on or near the road; take extra care around workers.
Do not pass; the sign creates an orderly flow before a merge or where passing is unsafe.
Drive to the right of this sign or obstruction.
Prepare for unusual road conditions or construction traffic moving at a different speed.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, survey work is being done on or near the road; take extra care around workers.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What does this sign indicate?
imageAlt: Orange work-zone sign warning of tar on the road

options.text: The road will narrow without reducing the number of lanes; slow because lane width is reduced.
Tar has been sprayed on the road ahead; the surface may be slippery and tar may stick to your vehicle.
Survey work is being done on or near the road; take extra care around workers.
The road has returned to normal and the work-zone impact has ended.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, tar has been sprayed on the road ahead; the surface may be slippery and tar may stick to your vehicle.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Orange end-of-road-work sign

options.text: Workers are handling overhead electrical or data cables near the road.
Workers are active at the site; drive with extra care.
The road has returned to normal and the work-zone impact has ended.
The lane ahead is closed or obstructed; move over and pass only on the side shown by the arrow.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, the road has returned to normal and the work-zone impact has ended.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What action does this sign require?
imageAlt: Orange combination sign warning of traffic control persons ahead

options.text: Passing is permitted only when the broken yellow line is on your side and conditions are safe.
Passing with care is permitted in both directions when the centre line is broken.
A bump or rough road is ahead; slow down.
Traffic may be stopped by traffic control persons ahead; slow gradually to avoid rear-end collisions.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, traffic may be stopped by traffic control persons ahead; slow gradually to avoid rear-end collisions.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What does this sign mean?
imageAlt: Yellow advance railway crossing warning sign

options.text: A railway crossbuck sign is ahead.
A fire truck entrance is ahead; watch for fire trucks entering or leaving.
Hazard marker lines slope left; drive to the left of the sign.
Hazard marker lines slope right; drive to the right of the sign.

explanation: Chapter 3 says railway signs warn of tracks and crossings where drivers must slow, look, listen, and be ready to stop. In this case, a railway crossbuck sign is ahead.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Railway Signs

text: What does this sign indicate?
imageAlt: Lane-use sign with straight-through and right-turn arrows

options.text: The maximum speed limit changes ahead; be ready to adjust your speed.
Drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn right.
Chevron arrows guide drivers around a sharp curve.
A climbing lane lets faster traffic pass slower vehicles; keep right unless passing.

explanation: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn right.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Orange human-activity sign for traffic control persons

options.text: Hazard marker lines slope left; drive to the left of the sign.
Hazard marker lines slope right; drive to the right of the sign.
Traffic control persons are using Stop/Slow paddles to manage traffic through the workplace.
A hidden intersection is ahead; side-road drivers may not clearly see approaching traffic.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, traffic control persons are using Stop/Slow paddles to manage traffic through the workplace.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What action does this sign require?
imageAlt: Yellow traffic-signal-lights-ahead warning sign

options.text: The smaller railway sign shows how many tracks must be crossed.
Keep to the right of the traffic island or obstruction.
A straight bar of lamps is caution mode, warning of a hazard without saying it is safe to pass.
Traffic signal lights are ahead.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, traffic signal lights are ahead.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What does this sign mean?
imageAlt: Yellow truck-entering warning sign

options.text: Trucks may be entering or leaving the road ahead.
No left turn is permitted.
No parking is allowed in either direction from this sign.
Do not turn right on a red signal; wait for a green light.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, trucks may be entering or leaving the road ahead.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What does this sign indicate?
imageAlt: Truck route sign with directional arrows

options.text: A solid yellow line marks the left edge of a divided highway; seeing it on the wrong side may mean you are travelling the wrong way.
Truck traffic may proceed only in the direction shown by the arrows.
A low-clearance underpass is ahead; check that your vehicle can pass under it.
Prepare to merge with other vehicles.

explanation: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, truck traffic may proceed only in the direction shown by the arrows.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Truck route regulatory sign

options.text: Prepare for unusual road conditions or construction traffic moving at a different speed.
A right curve is ahead and the posted advisory speed indicates a safe speed for the turn.
This road is a designated truck route.
The right lane ends ahead; merge left as soon as it is safe.

explanation: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, this road is a designated truck route.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

text: What does this pavement marking mean?
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram for a two-way left-turn lane

options.text: The road may be slippery when wet.
A steep decline is ahead; you may need to use a lower gear.
A stop sign is ahead.
The centre lane is reserved for left-turning vehicles from both directions; do not use it to pass.

explanation: Chapter 3 says pavement markings divide lanes, show permitted movements, and warn when passing or lane changes are unsafe. In this case, the centre lane is reserved for left-turning vehicles from both directions; do not use it to pass.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

text: What does this sign mean?
imageAlt: Yellow two-way-traffic warning sign

options.text: Traffic travels in both directions on this section of road.
Come to a complete stop and proceed only when the way is clear.
Drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn left.
Survey work is being done on or near the road; take extra care around workers.

explanation: Chapter 3 explains that warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead. In this case, traffic travels in both directions on this section of road.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

text: What does this sign indicate?
imageAlt: Orange human-activity sign for overhead cable work

options.text: Drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn left.
Workers are handling overhead electrical or data cables near the road.
A railway crossbuck sign is ahead.
Drivers in this lane must go straight through or turn right.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, workers are handling overhead electrical or data cables near the road.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What should you expect when you see this sign?
imageAlt: Orange human-activity sign showing workers active

options.text: Truck traffic may proceed only in the direction shown by the arrows.
This road is a designated truck route.
Workers are active at the site; drive with extra care.
The centre lane is reserved for left-turning vehicles from both directions; do not use it to pass.

explanation: Chapter 3 says work-zone signs warn of temporary conditions and require drivers to slow down and act early. In this case, workers are active at the site; drive with extra care.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

text: What action does this sign require?
imageAlt: Red and white triangular yield sign

options.text: Do not enter this road or lane.
Do not litter from your vehicle or along the roadway.
Do not pass other vehicles.
Reduce speed, give the right of way, and stop if necessary before proceeding.

explanation: Chapter 3 says regulatory signs state laws or rules drivers must obey. In this case, reduce speed, give the right of way, and stop if necessary before proceeding.
